content.problemStatement: In this task, we will create a program that formats an email address by removing unnecessary spaces. This helps practice basic string methods like `.strip()` and `.replace() for cleaning user input.
constraints: S consists of printable ASCII characters; spaces are the only whitespace to handle.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 100), the number of email addresses to format. Each of the next T lines contains a string S representing an email address. S may contain leading, trailing, or internal spaces and its length does not exceed 100 characters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a line in the exact form: "Formatted email: <formatted_email>" where <formatted_email> is S after removing leading and trailing spaces and replacing every remaining space with an underscore ('_').

sampleTestCases.output:
Formatted email: john.doe_@example.com
Formatted email: jane_doe123_@gmail.com
sampleTestCases.explanation: Each line is trimmed (no leading/trailing spaces) and the internal space before '@' is replaced by an underscore.
Leading and trailing spaces are removed, the remaining space becomes an underscore.
All three internal spaces become three underscores; the space before '@' becomes one underscore.

solution.approach: Read the number of test cases T. For each test case read the whole line (including spaces). First apply the standard trim operation to delete leading and trailing spaces. Then scan the trimmed string and replace every remaining space character with an underscore. Finally print the required prefix followed by the cleaned string. The algorithm works in linear time with respect to the total number of characters across all test cases and uses O(1) additional memory per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(total length of all input strings) – each character is inspected a constant number of times.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input string itself.
